english: pai sems (pa'i sems), and adj. faithful, believing, yón-bdag dad-pa (yon bdag dad pa) the faithful giver of alms Mil.; more fully dád(-pa)-càn (dad pa can), dad-ldán (dad ldan); ma-dád-pa (ma dad pa), and dad-méd (dad med) unbelieving; often with mos (mos) or gus (gus): kun dad-dad-mos-mós-su dúg-pa-la (kun dad dad mos mos su dug pa la) Mil.; dad-ćiñ-gus-par gyur-ba (dad cing gus par gyur ba) Glr.; dad-par gyúr-ba (dad par gyur ba), dád-pa byéd-pa (dad pa byed pa) to become faithful or believing, to believe, frq.; dád-bźin-du (dad bzhin du) full of faith; dad-brtsón (dad brtson) for dád-pa dañ brtson-grús (dad pa dang brtson grus) Tar. — Note. *mi źig-la dád-pa ťob* (mi zhig la dad pa thob) W. col. a man becomes a believer, v. ťób-pa (thob pa); but Tar. 35, 1 ṗágs-pa (phags pa) Dhi-ti-ka-la (dhi ti ka la) dád-pa ťob (dad pa thob) means: he was brought to believe by hearing the Reverend Dhitika.
dán-da (danda), and dan-róg (dan rog), medicinal herbs Med.
or dan-dál (dan dal), Ld. a sieve, gen. consisting of perforated leather and a wooden frame; rás-dan-dal (ras dan dal) a sieve made of cloth (inst. of leather).
(spelling?) the female of the ibex, and of the musk-deer.

(a root signifying bound, fast, fixed, from which the following compounds, as well as sdóm-pa (sdom pa), are to be derived), sbst., also dam-tsíg (dam tshig) and yi(d)-dam (yid dam), resp. ťugs-dam (thugs dam), a solemn promise; vow, oath, confirmation by oath, like bden-tsíg (bden tshig); dam bcà-ba (dam bca' ba) 1. to promise, 2. the act of promising, the promise; also dám-bcà (dam bca') Mil. and col.; dám-bcà °búl-ba (dam bca' 'bul ba) resp. to make a promise, e. g. mi °báb-pai (mi 'bab pa'i) not to descend Mil.; to promise solemnly Mil.; hence yi-dam (yi dam), and (more popularly) dám-bcà (dam bca') the sacrament Chr. Prot.; dam bcás-pa (dam bcas pa) a promise made; dam srúñ-ba (dam srung ba), dám-la gnás-pa (dam la gnas pa), or nyé-bar byéd-pa (nye bar byed pa), dám-bcas-pa spyód-pa (dam bcas pa spyod pa), dám-bcas-pa bźin-du byéd-pa (dam bcas pa bzhin du byed pa), dám-bcas-pa dañ mi °gál-ba (dam bcas pa dang mi 'gal ba), to keep one's promise; nyáms-pa (nyams pa) to break (a promise, a vow); dam-nyáms-kyi lás-rnams (dam nyams kyi las rnams) violations of duty; dám-la °dógs-pa (dam la 'dogs pa) to exorcise demons etc. Glr., Pth., but only by gentle persuasion, which induces them to promise to do no harm any more, not by magic power (so it was expressly stated by a Lama); dám-la °jóg-pa (dam la 'jog pa) Tar. 125 id. (ni f.); dám-càn (dam can), dam-tsíg-càn (dam tshig can) Mil. bound by an oath etc.; dám-ču (dam chu) prob. water which is drunk in taking an oath Pth.

Glr., dám-ga (dam ga) Wts., tám-ga (tam ga) Cs., a seal, stamp, resp. p'yag-dám (phyag dam), esp. for the seals of Lamas; dám-ka rgyáb-pa (dam ka rgyab pa) to seal, to stamp; k'yi-dam (khyi dam) v. k'yi (khyi); dam-rgyá (dam rgya) = dám-ka (dam ka) Tar.; *dam-cùg* (dam cug) W. seal of a Lama, used as an amulet.

acc. to the explanation of a Lama: bound by an oath or vow, consecrated; but Lexx. render it by परम, अग्र्य i. e. = mčog (mchog), thus Dzl. 23V, 4; 32, 9, and Cs.: noble, brave, excellent, which is prob. also the sense of the word when compounded with čos (chos), skyés-bu (skyes bu), and other words. Its usual rendering, however, is 2. holy, sacred, blá-ma dám-pa (bla ma dam pa), skyés-bu dám-pa (skyes bu dam pa), a holy Lama, a holy man, and most frq. dám-pai čos (dam pa'i chos), dám-pa čos (dam pa chos), dám-čos (dam chos), the holy doctrine, the holy religion of Buddha. Yet, in the interpretation of passages the original meaning (noble, excellent) ought to be resorted to much oftener. So also gyóg-mo dám-pa cig (g.yog mo dam pa cig) Glr. signifies an excellent, a favourite female slave, but not exactly a holy or a faithful one.
1. strong, firm; tight, narrow, of fetters etc.; gen. adverbially dam-du (dam du), e.g. to bind, to lock up, to seize firmly, securely. — 2. of laws, commandments, severe, strict, exact.

dar (dar) I. 1. silk, dár-gyi (dar gyi) of silk, silken; mjal-dár (mjal dar) resp. for k'a-btágs (kha btags) C.; rgyaï nañ dar (rgya'i nang dar) fine Chinese silks Thgy. — dar-dkár (dar dkar) white silk Glr. — dar-skúd (dar skud) silk-thread; gos-méd dar-skúd °dra (gos med dar skud 'dra) stark naked Ma. — dar-gós (dar gos) silk dress, Cs. also silk-stuff. — dar-čún (dar chun) a bunch or fringe of silk Cs. — dar-čén (dar chen) Ld.-Glr., acc. to Schl. = k'a-btágs (kha btags), yet cf. the significations given sub I. 2. — dar-°t'ág-mk'an (dar 'thag mkhan) a silk-weaver; dar-°t'ag-bu-mo (dar 'thag bu mo) Glr. the daughter of a silk-weaver. — dar-pón (dar pon) = dar-čún (dar chun). — dár-bu (dar bu) a coarse kind of silk Cs. — dar-búbs (dar bubs) a whole piece of silk-stuff rolled together. — *dhar-ma-rè'* (dhar ma re) C. 'neither silk nor cotton', half silk half
